questions.specification:
An object is placed into the top of a long vertical tube filled with a thick viscous fluid at time $t = 0$ seconds.

Initially, it is thought that the resistance of the fluid would be proportional to the velocity of the object. The following model was proposed, where the object’s displacement, $x$, from the top of the tube, measured in metres, is given by the differential equation

$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)$$

The maximum velocity approached by the object as it falls is known as the terminal velocity.

An experiment is performed in which the object is placed in the fluid on a number of occasions and its terminal velocity recorded. It is found that the terminal velocity was consistently smaller than that predicted by the model used. It was suggested that the resistance to motion is actually proportional to the velocity squared and so the following model was set up:

$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

parts.content: By substituting $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ into the equation, find an expression for the velocity of the object at time $t$. Give your answer in the form $v = f(t)$.
parts.markscheme:
$$\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9v$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$\int \frac{1}{9.81 - 0.9v} \text{d}v = \int 1 \, \text{d}t$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$-\frac{1}{0.9}\ln(9.81 - 0.9v) = t + c$$ <marks>A1</marks>

$$9.81 - 0.9v = Ae^{-0.9t}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

$$v = \frac{9.81 - Ae^{-0.9t}}{0.9}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

When $t = 0, v = 0$, hence $A = 9.81$. <marks>A1</marks>

$$v = \frac{9.81(1 - e^{-0.9t})}{0.9}$$

$$v = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the velocity equation
steps.content:
First, we need to transform our second-order differential equation into a first-order one using the substitution $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. Since velocity is the rate of change of displacement, acceleration is the rate of change of velocity: 
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Substituting these into the model provided in the question, we get:
$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v$$

steps.title: Separate the variables
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Separation of Variables** method (Topic 5.14), we move all terms involving $v$ to one side and all terms involving $t$ to the other. 

We divide both sides by $(9.81 - 0.9v)$ and multiply by $dt$:
$$\frac{1}{9.81 - 0.9v} dv = 1 dt$$

steps.title: Integrate both sides
steps.content:
Now, we integrate both sides of the equation:
$$\int \frac{1}{9.81 - 0.9v} dv = \int 1 dt$$

The right side is simple: $\int 1 dt = t + C$. 

For the left side, we use the rule $\int \frac{1}{ax+b} dx = \frac{1}{a} \ln|ax+b|$:
$$-\frac{1}{0.9} \ln(9.81 - 0.9v) = t + C$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity algebraically
steps.content:
We need to isolate $v$. Multiply by $-0.9$ and then exponentiate both sides:
$$\ln(9.81 - 0.9v) = -0.9(t + C)$$
$$9.81 - 0.9v = e^{-0.9t - 0.9C}$$

We can let $A = e^{-0.9C}$ represent our new arbitrary constant:
$$9.81 - 0.9v = Ae^{-0.9t}$$
$$0.9v = 9.81 - Ae^{-0.9t}$$

steps.title: Apply initial conditions
steps.content:
When the object is first **placed** into the tube at $t = 0$, its initial velocity is $v = 0$. We use this to find the value of $A$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9(0) &= 9.81 - Ae^{-0.9(0)} \\ 0 &= 9.81 - A(1) \\ A &= 9.81 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $A$ back into our equation:

$$0.9v = 9.81 - 9.81e^{-0.9t}$$

highlights.text: placed into the top of a long vertical tube filled with a thick viscous fluid at time $t = 0$ seconds.

steps.title: Final expression for v
steps.content:
Finally, divide by $0.9$ to isolate $v$:
$$v = \frac{9.81(1 - e^{-0.9t})}{0.9}$$

Using a calculator to find $9.81 / 0.9$:
$$v = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 9.81 / 0.9 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 9.81/0.9 in an expression cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient for the velocity function.

steps.title: Define the first-order equation
steps.content:
First, we use the substitution $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. This means the second derivative of displacement, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$, becomes the first derivative of velocity, $\frac{dv}{dt}$. 

Substituting this into the given differential equation:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v$$

steps.title: Rearrange to standard form
steps.content:
To use the Integrating Factor Method, we need to rearrange the equation into the standard linear form: 
$$\frac{dv}{dt} + P(t)v = Q(t)$$

By adding $0.9v$ to both sides, we get:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} + 0.9v = 9.81$$

steps.title: Find the integrating factor
steps.content:
The integrating factor, $I(t)$, is found using the formula $e^{\int P(t) \, dt}$. Here, $P(t) = 0.9$.

$$\begin{aligned} I(t) &= e^{\int 0.9 \, dt} \\ &= e^{0.9t} \end{aligned}$$

(We omit the constant of integration here as it cancels out later).

steps.title: Multiply and integrate
steps.content:
We now multiply every term in our standard form equation by the integrating factor $e^{0.9t}$:

$$e^{0.9t} \frac{dv}{dt} + 0.9e^{0.9t} v = 9.81e^{0.9t}$$

The left-hand side is now the derivative of the product $(v \cdot e^{0.9t})$ by the product rule:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(v e^{0.9t}) = 9.81e^{0.9t}$$

Now, integrate both sides with respect to $t$:

$$v e^{0.9t} = \int 9.81e^{0.9t} \, dt$$

steps.title: Evaluate the integral
steps.content:
Perform the integration on the right-hand side using the rule $\int e^{kt} \, dt = \frac{1}{k}e^{kt} + C$:

$$\begin{aligned} v e^{0.9t} &= \frac{9.81}{0.9}e^{0.9t} + C \\ v e^{0.9t} &= 10.9e^{0.9t} + C \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 9.81 / 0.9 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press 9.81, then divide symbol, then 0.9, then ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 9.81 / 0.9 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient 9.81 divided by 0.9.

steps.title: Solve for the constant
steps.content:
The object is placed in the tube at $t = 0$, so its initial velocity is zero ($v = 0$ at $t = 0$). Substitute these values to find $C$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 \cdot e^{0} &= 10.9e^{0} + C \\ 0 &= 10.9(1) + C \\ C &= -10.9 \end{aligned}$$

Substitute $C$ back into the general solution:

$$v e^{0.9t} = 10.9e^{0.9t} - 10.9$$

steps.title: Final expression for velocity
steps.content:
To isolate $v$, divide every term by $e^{0.9t}$ (which is the same as multiplying by $e^{-0.9t}$):

$$v = 10.9 - 10.9e^{-0.9t}$$

Factorizing the result gives the final form $v = f(t)$:

$$v = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

parts.content: From your solution to part 1, or otherwise, find the terminal velocity of the object predicted by this model.
parts.markscheme:
**either** let $t$ tend to infinity, or $\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} = 0$ <marks>(M1)</marks>

$v = 10.9$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is the constant maximum velocity reached by an object falling through a fluid. Because the velocity is constant at this stage, the object is no longer accelerating. 

Mathematically, this means the second derivative of displacement (acceleration) must be zero:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 0$$

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Apply the model
steps.content:
We are asked to find the terminal velocity for the second model, where resistance is proportional to velocity squared. This model is given by:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{dx}{dt} \right)^2$$

To find the terminal velocity ($v$), we substitute $0$ for the acceleration and $v$ for the velocity $\frac{dx}{dt}$ into the equation:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity
steps.content:
Now we solve the algebraic equation for $v$. First, isolate the $v^2$ term:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v^2 &= 10.9 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, take the square root of both sides to find the value of $v$:

$$v = \sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.30 \text{ m/s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter sqrt(10.9) in the main calculation menu and press EXE/ENTER.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x"] (for ∑), then type 10.9 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(10.9) in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root of 10.9 to find the terminal velocity.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The terminal velocity predicted by the second model is approximately $3.30 \text{ m/s}$ (to 3 significant figures). 

Note: In the IB markscheme, you would earn one mark for stating the condition that acceleration equals zero and one mark for the final numerical value.

steps.title: Identify the velocity function
steps.content:
In Part 1, you solved the differential equation for the initial model to find the velocity as a function of time, $v(t)$. 

The general solution for an object starting from rest in that model is:

$$v(t) = 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

This function describes how the object's speed increases over time, eventually leveling off as it reaches a steady state.

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is defined as the maximum speed an object approaches as it falls through a fluid. Mathematically, this corresponds to the limit of the velocity function as time, $t$, tends toward infinity.

$$v_{\text{terminal}} = \lim_{t \to \infty} v(t)$$

highlights.text: The maximum velocity approached by the object as it falls is known as the terminal velocity.
either let t tend to infinity

steps.title: Evaluate the limit
steps.content:
We now evaluate the limit for our function:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} 10.9(1 - e^{-0.9t})$$

As $t$ becomes extremely large, the term $e^{-0.9t}$ (which is $\frac{1}{e^{0.9t}}$) approaches zero. This leaves us with:

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{terminal}} &= 10.9(1 - 0) \\ v_{\text{terminal}} &= 10.9 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

The terminal velocity predicted by the model from Part 1 is $10.9 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the object is no longer accelerating. This means the net force is zero, and therefore the acceleration, $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$, is zero.

From the question, we are given the model for the second experiment:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up graphical function
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity graphically, we treat acceleration as our dependent variable ($y$) and velocity as our independent variable ($x$). 

Let $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ be represented by $x$ and $a = \frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$ be represented by $y$. We need to find the value of $x$ when $y = 0$ for the following function:

$$y = 9.81 - 0.9x^2$$

steps.title: Find the x-intercept
steps.content:
We can now use the graphing features of a calculator to identify the $x$-intercept (the root) of this function where $x > 0$. 

Note: In the first model, the terminal velocity was $10.9 \text{ m/s}$. For this model, we are looking for the velocity $v$ such that $v^2 = 10.9$.

steps.title: State terminal velocity
steps.content:
The calculator identifies the $x$-intercept at $x \approx 3.3015...$. 

Following the IB standard of rounding to 3 significant figures, we get:

$$v_t = 3.30 \text{ m/s}$$

This is consistent with the experimental finding that the terminal velocity was smaller than the $10.9 \text{ m/s}$ predicted by the first model.

parts.content: Write down the differential equation for the second model as a system of first-order differential equations.
parts.markscheme:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = y$$ <marks>M1</marks>

$$\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9y^2$$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the substitution method
steps.content:
To convert a second-order differential equation into a system of two first-order equations, we need to introduce a new variable. Usually, we let this new variable represent the first derivative (velocity). 

In this problem, the second model is given as:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Define the first equation
steps.content:
Let's define a new variable, $y$, to represent the velocity of the object. Velocity is the rate of change of displacement, $x$, with respect to time, $t$.

Our first first-order differential equation is:
$$\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = y$$

This accounts for the first mark ($M1$) in the markscheme.

steps.title: Substitute into the model
steps.content:
Next, we look at the second derivative, $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$, which is the derivative of the first derivative. Since we defined $y = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$, then:
$$\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = \frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$$

Now, we substitute $y$ and $\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t}$ back into the second model equation:
$$\frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9y^2$$

This provides the second mark ($A1$).

steps.title: State the final system
steps.content:
By combining the two equations we've found, we obtain the coupled system of first-order differential equations for the second model:

$$\begin{cases} \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = y \\ \frac{\text{d}y}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9y^2 \end{cases}$$

This method is a prerequisite for numerical methods like **Euler's method for second-order DEs**, which you can find in **Topic 5: AHL 5.18**.

parts.content: Use Euler’s method, with a step length of $0.2$, to find the displacement and velocity of the object when $t = 0.6$.

steps.title: Set up recurrence relations
steps.content:
To apply Euler's method to a second-order differential equation manually, we treat it as a system of two first-order equations. We let velocity $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$. 

From the question:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

Substituting $v$, our system of rates is:
1. $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} = v$
2. $\frac{\text{d}v}{\text{d}t} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$

Using the step length $h=0.2$, the Euler recurrence relations for displacement ($x$) and velocity ($v$) are:
$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.2 v_n \\ v_{n+1} &= v_n + 0.2 (9.81 - 0.9v_n^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: First iteration (t=0 to 0.2)
steps.content:
We start at $t=0$ with $x_0 = 0$ and $v_0 = 0$. We calculate the values for $t=0.2$ ($n=1$):

- **Displacement ($x_1$):** 
$$x_1 = x_0 + 0.2(v_0) = 0 + 0.2(0) = 0$$

- **Velocity ($v_1$):** 
$$v_1 = v_0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9v_0^2) = 0 + 0.2(9.81 - 0) = 1.962$$

So at $t=0.2$, the object has fallen $0$ m (as per the model's first step) and has a velocity of $1.962$ m/s.

steps.title: Convert to first-order system
steps.content:
To apply Euler's method to a second-order differential equation, we first convert it into a system of two first-order equations. Let $v$ be the velocity, defined as $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. 

The original equation is:
$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{dx}{dt} \right)^2$$

Substituting $v$ for $\frac{dx}{dt}$, we get the system:
$$\begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Define Euler's recurrence relations
steps.content:
Using Euler's method with a step length of $h = 0.2$, we can write recurrence relations for the displacement ($x$) and velocity ($v$). 

For each step $n$:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot v_n$$
$$v_{n+1} = v_n + h \cdot (9.81 - 0.9v_n^2)$$

Substituting $h = 0.2$, our coupled relations are:
$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.2v_n \\ v_{n+1} &= v_n + 0.2(9.81 - 0.9v_n^2) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Setup GDC Sequence Tool

3. Set initial values: x_0 = 0 and v_0 = 0.
4. View the table and find the values at n = 3.

parts.content: By repeated application of Euler’s method, find an approximation for the terminal velocity, to five significant figures.
parts.markscheme: $3.3015$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the differential equation
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity using Euler's method, we first need to express the model as a first-order differential equation. Let the velocity be $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. The second derivative $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ represents acceleration, so we can rewrite the equation as:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

This is the function $f(t, v)$ that we will use in Euler's formula.

steps.title: Set up Euler's formula
steps.content:
Euler's method approximates the next value in a sequence using the formula $v_{n+1} = v_n + h \times f(t_n, v_n)$, where $h$ is a small step size. In our case:

$$v_{next} = v + h(9.81 - 0.9v^2)$$

Since we want to find the terminal velocity (the value $v$ approaches as $t \to \infty$), we can pick a small step size like $h = 0.1$ and iterate until the velocity stops changing significantly. We start with $v = 0$ at $t = 0$.

steps.title: Recursive iteration on calculator
steps.content:
We can use the **Ans** feature on your calculator to perform this quickly. We first store the initial velocity, then enter the recursive formula. 

Each time you press **Enter**, the calculator computes the next velocity step. We keep going until the value stabilizes to five significant figures.

gdc.instructions:

2. Enter the recursion: 'Ans + 0.1(9.81 - 0.9Ans^2)' [EXE].
3. Press [EXE] repeatedly until the value converges.

ti84.instructions:

2. Type 'Ans + 0.1 * (9.81 - 0.9 * Ans^2)' and press [ENTER].
3. Repeatedly press [ENTER] until the value converges to five significant figures.

steps.title: Determine terminal velocity
steps.content:
As you continue to iterate, you will notice the velocity values get closer and closer to a specific number. This happens because as $v$ increases, the acceleration $(9.81 - 0.9v^2)$ decreases toward zero.

After many iterations, the value converges to:

$$v \approx 3.3015$$

To verify, we can check the equilibrium condition where acceleration is zero:
$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}} = \sqrt{10.9}$$
Calculating $\sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.301514...$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding the converged value to five significant figures, we get:

$$v_t = 3.3015 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify the velocity model
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity using Euler's method, we first need to express the given second-order differential equation in terms of velocity, $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$. 

Since acceleration is the derivative of velocity, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dv}{dt}$. We can rewrite the model as:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Set up Euler's recurrence
steps.content:
Euler's method approximates the next value in a sequence using the formula $v_{n} = v_{n-1} + h \cdot f(v_{n-1})$, where $h$ is a small step size in time and $f(v) = \frac{dv}{dt}$. 

Substituting our model into this recursive formula, we get:

$$v_{n} = v_{n-1} + h(9.81 - 0.9v_{n-1}^2)$$

We start at $t = 0$ with an initial velocity $v_0 = 0$.

steps.title: Configure GDC sequence mode
steps.content: We can use the Sequence mode or a Spreadsheet on your GDC to calculate many iterations quickly. By choosing a small step size (e.g., $h = 0.1$), we can observe the values of $v_n$ as $n$ increases.

steps.title: Identify the terminal value
steps.content:
The terminal velocity is reached when the acceleration becomes zero, meaning the sequence $v_n$ stops changing. Looking at the GDC table, as $n$ becomes very large, the velocity converges to a constant value.

Using the calculator tool, we can verify the exact square root value that the sequence is approaching:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \Rightarrow v = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}}$$

steps.title: Define the first-order system
steps.content:
To solve a second-order differential equation using Euler's method, we first rewrite it as a system of two coupled first-order equations. 

Let $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$ be the velocity. Then, the acceleration is $\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$. Substituting this into our model, we get the following system:

$$\begin{cases} \frac{dx}{dt} = v \\ \frac{dv}{dt} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2 \end{cases}$$

This is known as a coupled system because the rate of change of $v$ depends on $v$ itself, and the rate of change of $x$ depends on $v$.

steps.title: Set up Euler's formulas
steps.content:
Euler's method uses a small time step, $h$, to approximate the next value in a sequence based on the current gradient. For our coupled system, the update rules are:

$$v_{n+1} = v_n + h \times (9.81 - 0.9v_n^2)$$
$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \times v_n$$

At $t=0$, the object is placed into the tube, so we assume initial conditions of $v_0 = 0$ and $x_0 = 0$.

highlights.text: By repeated application of Euler’s method

steps.title: Iterate using technology
steps.content:
Terminal velocity occurs when the velocity $v$ approaches a constant value as $t$ (and therefore $n$) increases. We can use a Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to iterate this sequence many times until the value of $v_n$ stabilizes.

Using a small step size like $h = 0.1$, the first few steps look like this:
1. $v_1 = 0 + 0.1(9.81 - 0.9(0)^2) = 0.981$
2. $v_2 = 0.981 + 0.1(9.81 - 0.9(0.981)^2) \approx 1.8753$

By repeating this process hundreds of times (or using the calculator's sequence tools), $v_n$ will converge to the terminal velocity.

As $n \to \infty$, the velocity $v_n$ approaches a steady state where the acceleration $9.81 - 0.9v^2$ effectively becomes zero.

This matches the experimental observation that the terminal velocity is smaller than predicted by the first model (which would have been $9.81 / 0.9 = 10.9$ m s$^{-1}$).

steps.title: Identify terminal velocity condition
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the forces acting on the falling object are perfectly balanced, meaning the object is no longer speeding up or slowing down. In mathematical terms, this occurs when the acceleration is zero.

From the differential equation provided for the second model:
$$\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9\left( \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t} \right)^2$$

We know that $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2}$ represents acceleration and $\frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$ represents velocity ($v$).

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up the algebraic equation
steps.content:
To find the terminal velocity ($v$), we set the acceleration term to zero in our model:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

steps.title: Solve for velocity
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to isolate $v^2$ and then solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.9}} \end{aligned}$$

By simplifying the fraction inside the square root:
$$\frac{9.81}{0.9} = 10.9$$

So,
$$v = \sqrt{10.9}$$

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity is reached when the object stops accelerating and maintains a constant speed. The question confirms this by stating that at terminal velocity, the acceleration is zero.

In our differential equation, $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ represents acceleration ($a$) and $\frac{dx}{dt}$ represents velocity ($v$). We are looking for the value of $v$ when:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = 0$$

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up the function
steps.content:
Using the second model provided in the question, we substitute $0$ for the acceleration term to find the velocity $v$:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

To solve this graphically, we can treat the right side of the equation as a function where $y$ is the acceleration and $x$ is the velocity:

$$f(x) = 9.81 - 0.9x^2$$

steps.title: Graph and find intercept
steps.content: We now use a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) to plot the function and identify the positive $x$-intercept (where $y = 0$). Since velocity in this context is downward and modelled as a positive value, we ignore the negative root.

steps.title: State the exact velocity
steps.content:
The GDC identifies the intercept at approximately $x = 3.30$. 

To find the **exact** value, we solve for $v$ algebraically:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v^2 &= 10.9 \\ v &= \sqrt{10.9} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the terminal velocity is exactly $\sqrt{10.9} \text{ m s}^{-1}$, which is approximately $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ to three significant figures.

parts.content: Use your answers to parts 4, 5 and 6 to comment on the accuracy of the Euler approximation to this model.
parts.markscheme:
The model found the terminal velocity very accurately, so it is a good approximation. <marks>R1</marks>

However, intermediate values had the object exceeding terminal velocity (e.g., at $t=0.6$), so it is not a perfect approximation in the short term. <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Calculate theoretical terminal velocity
steps.content:
To evaluate the accuracy of the Euler approximation, we first need to know the 'true' limit the object should reach. The question states that at terminal velocity, acceleration is zero. Let $v = \frac{\text{d}x}{\text{d}t}$. We set the acceleration equation to zero:

$$0 = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$$

Solving for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.9v^2 &= 9.81 \\ v^2 &= \frac{9.81}{0.9} \\ v^2 &= 10.9 \\ v &= \sqrt{10.9} \approx 3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This is the theoretical value that our numerical approximation should approach.

highlights.text: At terminal velocity, the acceleration of an object is equal to zero.

steps.title: Evaluate long-term convergence
steps.content:
From your results in previous parts, you likely found that as $t$ increases, the velocity values produced by Euler's method settle very close to $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

Because the numerical values eventually align with the theoretical terminal velocity, we can say the model is a **good approximation** for the long-term behavior of the object.

steps.title: Identify short-term inconsistencies
steps.content:
Now we look at the short-term accuracy. In step-by-step methods like Euler's, if the step size is too large, the approximation can 'overshoot' the true curve. 

According to the markscheme, at an intermediate time (like $t=0.6$), the calculated velocity actually **exceeded** the terminal velocity of $3.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. In reality, the object cannot fall faster than its terminal velocity in this model, because as soon as $v > 3.30$, the acceleration $\frac{\text{d}^2x}{\text{d}t^2} = 9.81 - 0.9v^2$ becomes negative, slowing it back down immediately.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, your comment should reflect two points:

1.  **Accuracy:** The approximation is good because it predicts the terminal velocity very accurately in the long run.
2.  **Limitations:** It is not a perfect approximation because of the physical inconsistency where the object exceeds terminal velocity in the short term.

parts.content: Using Euler's method with a step size $h = 0.2$, approximate $y(0.4)$.
parts.markscheme:
* Attempt to use the Euler's method formula: <marks>M1</marks>

$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_n, y_n)$$

* Substitution of $t_0 = 0$ and $y_0 = 2$ to find the gradient at the start: $f(0, 2) = 3(2) - 4(0) = 6$ <marks>M1</marks>
* Calculation of the first step for $y(0.2)$: $y_1 = 2 + 0.2(6) = 3.2$ <marks>M1</marks>
* Substitution of $t_1 = 0.2$ and $y_1 = 3.2$ to find the gradient for the next step: $f(0.2, 3.2) = 3(3.2) - 4(0.2) = 8.8$ <marks>M1</marks>
* Substitution into the Euler's formula for the second step: $y_2 = 3.2 + 0.2(8.8)$ <marks>M1</marks>
* Final value: $y(0.4) \approx 4.96$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Solve the differential equation to find the general solution for $C$ in terms of $t$.
parts.markscheme:
- Rearrange the differential equation into the standard linear form: $\frac{dC}{dt} + 0.2C = 5$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Determine the integrating factor: $e^{\int 0.2 dt} = e^{0.2t}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply the integrating factor: $C e^{0.2t} = \int 5 e^{0.2t} dt$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Obtain the general solution: $C e^{0.2t} = \frac{5}{0.2}e^{0.2t} + k \Rightarrow C = 25 + k e^{-0.2t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Use Euler's method with a step size of 0.5 months to estimate the concentration after 1 month.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the first step at $t = 0.5$: $C_1 = 10 + 0.5(-0.2 \cdot 10 + 5) = 11.5$ <marks>M1A1</marks>
- Calculate the second step at $t = 1.0$: $C_2 = 11.5 + 0.5(-0.2 \cdot 11.5 + 5) = 12.85\text{ mg/L}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand Euler's Method
steps.content:
To estimate the concentration, we use **Euler's Method**. This is an iterative process that uses the rate of change at a specific point to predict the next value. The general formula we'll use is:

$$C_{n+1} = C_n + h \times \frac{dC}{dt}$$

where:
- $h$ is the step size (0.5 months)
- $C_n$ is the current concentration
- $\frac{dC}{dt}$ is the rate of change at that concentration

This technique is a key part of **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.16)**.

steps.title: First Step: $t = 0.5$
steps.content:
We start at $t = 0$ with $C_0 = 10$. First, we calculate the rate of change at this starting point:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -0.2(10) + 5 = 3$$

Now, we apply the Euler formula to find the concentration at $t = 0.5$ ($C_1$):

$$\begin{aligned} C_1 &= C_0 + h \times \left( \frac{dC}{dt} \right)_{t=0} \\ C_1 &= 10 + 0.5(3) \\ C_1 &= 11.5 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned}$$

You earn **1 method mark** and **1 accuracy mark** for correctly reaching $11.5$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 10 + 0.5 * (-0.2 * 10 + 5) on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the concentration at the first step.

highlights.text: Initially, at t = 0, the concentration is C = 10 mg/L

steps.title: Second Step: $t = 1.0$
steps.content:
Now we use our new value, $C_1 = 11.5$, to estimate the concentration at $t = 1.0$ ($C_2$). 

First, calculate the new rate of change:
$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -0.2(11.5) + 5 = 2.7$$

Now, apply the Euler formula again:
$$\begin{aligned} C_2 &= C_1 + h \times \left( \frac{dC}{dt} \right)_{t=0.5} \\ C_2 &= 11.5 + 0.5(2.7) \\ C_2 &= 12.85 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned}$$

This final calculation earns the **3rd mark**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 11.5 + 0.5 * (-0.2 * 11.5 + 5) on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the concentration at the second step.

steps.content: The estimated concentration of the pollutant after 1 month is **$12.85 \text{ mg/L}$**.

steps.title: Define Euler's method variables
steps.content:
To solve this using a recursive approach, we first identify our starting conditions and the step size ($h$). We are given that the initial concentration is $10$ mg/L at $t=0$, and we need to find the concentration at $t=1$ month using a step size of $0.5$ months.

- Initial value: $C_0 = 10$
- Step size: $h = 0.5$
- Differential equation: $\frac{dC}{dt} = -0.2C + 5$

highlights.text: Initially, at $t = 0$, the concentration is $C = 10\text{ mg/L}$

steps.title: Formulate the recurrence relation
steps.content:
Euler's method can be expressed as a linear recurrence relation. The formula for the next value is the current value plus the step size multiplied by the rate of change:

$$C_{n+1} = C_n + h \times \left(\frac{dC}{dt}\right)_n$$

Substituting our specific rate equation and step size:

$$C_{n+1} = C_n + 0.5(-0.2C_n + 5)$$

steps.title: Simplify the recursive model
steps.content:
To make iterating easier on your calculator, simplify the expression by expanding the brackets:

$$\begin{aligned} C_{n+1} &= C_n + (0.5 \times -0.2C_n) + (0.5 \times 5) \\ C_{n+1} &= C_n - 0.1C_n + 2.5 \\ C_{n+1} &= 0.9C_n + 2.5 \end{aligned}$$

This simple model, $C_{n+1} = 0.9C_n + 2.5$, represents how the concentration changes every $0.5$ months.

steps.title: Calculate the first step
steps.content:
Now we perform the first iteration to find the concentration at $t = 0.5$ months ($C_1$). Use the initial value $C_0 = 10$:

$$C_1 = 0.9(10) + 2.5$$

$$C_1 = 11.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

This earns you the first two marks for showing the method and finding the intermediate value.

steps.title: Calculate the second step
steps.content:
To find the concentration after 1 month ($t = 1.0$), we iterate once more using $C_1 = 11.5$:

$$C_2 = 0.9(11.5) + 2.5$$

$$C_2 = 12.85 \text{ mg/L}$$

This provides the final estimate for the pollutant concentration.

steps.title: Find the analytical solution
steps.content:
To sketch the solution curve accurately, we first solve the differential equation analytically using **separation of variables**. Starting with the given rate of change:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -0.2C + 5$$

We can factor out $-0.2$ to make integration easier:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -0.2(C - 25)$$

Separating the variables $C$ and $t$:

$$\int \frac{1}{C - 25} \, dC = \int -0.2 \, dt$$

Integrating both sides gives:

$$\ln|C - 25| = -0.2t + k$$

Exponentiating both sides and using the initial condition $C(0) = 10$:

$$C - 25 = Ae^{-0.2t} \implies 10 - 25 = Ae^{0} \implies A = -15$$

So, the **particular solution** is:

$$C(t) = 25 - 15e^{-0.2t}$$

highlights.text: Initially, at $t = 0$, the concentration is $C = 10\text{ mg/L}$

steps.title: Determine the curve's concavity
steps.content:
To sketch correctly, we check the concavity by finding the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2C}{dt^2} = \frac{d}{dt}(-0.2C + 5) = -0.2\frac{dC}{dt}$$

Since $C$ increases from 10 toward the limit of 25, $\frac{dC}{dt}$ is positive. Therefore, $\frac{d^2C}{dt^2}$ is **negative**, meaning the curve is **concave down**. 

Because the curve is concave down, Euler's method (which uses tangent lines) will **overestimate** the actual values. On your graph, the straight line segments should lie slightly **above** the smooth solution curve.

steps.title: Sketch the final graph
steps.content:
When sketching for $0 \leq t \leq 5$:
1. **Solution Curve:** Draw a smooth curve starting at $(0, 10)$, passing through approximately $(5, 19.5)$, and gradually leveling off toward the horizontal asymptote $C = 25$.
2. **Euler Approximations:** Plot the specific points $(0, 10)$, $(0.5, 11.5)$, and $(1, 12.85)$. Connect these three points with **straight line segments**.
3. **Labels:** Ensure the $t$-axis (months) and $C$-axis (mg/L) are clearly labeled, along with the key points calculated.

steps.title: Calculate Euler points with GDC
steps.content:
Euler's method uses the gradient at a point to find the next point. To find the first two steps with a step size of $h=0.5$:

1. **Step 0**: $(t_0, C_0) = (0, 10)$
2. **Step 1**: 
$$\begin{aligned} C_1 &= C_0 + h \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} \\ &= 10 + 0.5(-0.2(10) + 5) \\ &= 11.5 \end{aligned}$$
Point: $(0.5, 11.5)$

3. **Step 2**:
$$\begin{aligned} C_2 &= C_1 + h \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} \\ &= 11.5 + 0.5(-0.2(11.5) + 5) \\ &= 12.85 \end{aligned}$$
Point: $(1, 12.85)$

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To determine if the lake is safe, we need to calculate the concentration of the pollutant $C$ at exactly $t = 10$ months and compare it to the safety threshold of $15 \text{ mg/L}$.

From the previous part of this problem, we found the particular solution for the concentration over time:

$$C = 25 - 15e^{-0.2t}$$

highlights.text: lake is considered safe when C < 15 mg/L

steps.title: Substitute the time value
steps.content:
We substitute $t = 10$ into our particular solution to find the specific concentration at that time:

$$C(10) = 25 - 15e^{-0.2(10)}$$

Simplifying the exponent:

$$C(10) = 25 - 15e^{-2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the concentration
steps.content:
Now, use your calculator to evaluate the expression. 

$$\begin{aligned} C(10) &= 25 - 15e^{-2} \\ C(10) &\approx 25 - 2.030... \\ C(10) &\approx 22.97 \text{ mg/L} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Finally, compare your calculated value to the safety threshold. 

Since $22.97 \text{ mg/L} > 15 \text{ mg/L}$, the concentration is higher than the allowed limit.

**Conclusion:** The lake is **not safe** after 10 months.

In an IB exam, you earn **one mark** for the correct numerical value of $C$ and **one mark** for the reasoning/comparison that leads to the conclusion.

questions.specification:

parts.content: Interpret the four terms on the right-hand sides of the differential equations in the context of the model.
parts.markscheme:
The terms are interpreted as follows:

$2R$: The natural growth rate of the rabbit population in the absence of foxes. <marks>A1</marks>

$-0.02RF$: The rate at which the rabbit population decreases due to predation by foxes. <marks>A1</marks>

$0.01RF$: The rate at which the fox population increases due to the consumption of rabbits. <marks>A1</marks>

$-0.5F$: The natural death rate of the fox population in the absence of rabbits. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Use Euler's method with a step size of $h=0.1$ years to estimate the populations of rabbits and foxes after 0.2 years.
Find the non-zero equilibrium point $(R, F)$ for this system.
parts.markscheme:
For equilibrium, set $\frac{dR}{dt} = 0$ and $\frac{dF}{dt} = 0$: <marks>M1</marks>

$R(2 - 0.02F) = 0 \Rightarrow F = \frac{2}{0.02} = 100$ <marks>A1</marks>

$F(0.01R - 0.5) = 0 \Rightarrow R = \frac{0.5}{0.01} = 50$ <marks>A1</marks>

The non-zero equilibrium point is $(50, 100)$.

steps.title: Define the equilibrium condition
steps.content:
An equilibrium point (or steady state) in a system of differential equations occurs when the populations are no longer changing over time. Mathematically, this means setting the rates of change to zero:

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dF}{dt} = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for F
steps.content:
Using the first equation for rabbits, we set it to zero and factor out the common variable $R$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2R - 0.02RF &= 0 \\ R(2 - 0.02F) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

For a **non-zero** equilibrium point, we assume $R \neq 0$. This leaves us with:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 - 0.02F &= 0 \\ 2 &= 0.02F \\ F &= \frac{2}{0.02} = 100 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 / 0.02 in the run-matrix mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 / 0.02 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 2 / 0.02 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the equilibrium value for F.

steps.title: Solve for R
steps.content:
Next, we use the second equation for foxes, setting it to zero and factoring out the common variable $F$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.01RF - 0.5F &= 0 \\ F(0.01R - 0.5) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since we are looking for the non-zero point, we assume $F \neq 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.01R - 0.5 &= 0 \\ 0.01R &= 0.5 \\ R &= \frac{0.5}{0.01} = 50 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 / 0.01 in the run-matrix mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.5 / 0.01 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.5 / 0.01 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the equilibrium value for R.

steps.title: State final equilibrium point
steps.content:
Combining our results, the non-zero equilibrium point $(R, F)$ is:

$$(50, 100)$$

This point represents a stable state where the rabbit population is $5,000$ and the fox population is $10,000$ (since units are in hundreds).

steps.title: Understand equilibrium
steps.content: In a predator-prey system, an **equilibrium point** occurs when the populations of both species are no longer changing over time. Mathematically, this means the rates of change, $\frac{dR}{dt}$ and $\frac{dF}{dt}$, must both be equal to zero.

steps.title: Set up the equations
steps.content:
We take the two differential equations given in the question and set them both to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} 2R - 0.02RF &= 0 \\ 0.01RF - 0.5F &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since we are looking for a **non-zero** equilibrium point, we assume $R \neq 0$ and $F \neq 0$.

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content:
Instead of solving these by hand, we can use the **Numeric Solver** or **System of Equations Solver** on your graphing calculator. 

We enter the equations into the solver, using $x$ for $R$ and $y$ for $F$. Since the question specifies a non-zero point, we can provide the calculator with a 'guess' or bound (like $R=10, F=10$) to help it avoid the trivial $(0,0)$ solution.

gdc.instructions: Go to Equation mode [EQUA], select Simultaneous [F1], and input the equations. Note: Some GDCs require rearranging to standard form ax + by = c, but for non-linear systems, the Solver [F3] tool is often best.

ti84.instructions: Go to [MATH] -> [Numeric Solver]. Enter Equation 1 as E1: 2*X - 0.02*X*Y and Equation 2 as E2: 0.01*X*Y - 0.5*Y. Set initial guesses for X and Y to 10 and press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the two equations into separate lines: 0 = 2R - 0.02RF and 0 = 0.01RF - 0.5F. The intersection point of the resulting lines on the graph (excluding the origin) is the equilibrium.

calculator.description: Solve the system of equations using the GDC numeric solver.

steps.title: Interpret the results
steps.content:
The calculator provides the following values:

$$\begin{aligned} R &= 50 \\ F &= 100 \end{aligned}$$

This means that if there are $5,000$ rabbits and $10,000$ foxes (remember the units are in hundreds), the populations will stay constant.

steps.title: State final coordinates
steps.content:
The non-zero equilibrium point is $(50, 100)$. 

In the IB exam, you earn marks for setting the derivatives to zero and correctly identifying the coordinates from your GDC.

steps.title: Define the Phase Portrait
steps.content:
A phase portrait for a predator-prey system shows the relationship between the two populations (Rabbits $R$ and Foxes $F$) over time, rather than plotting each against time individually. In this case, we will plot $R$ on the horizontal axis and $F$ on the vertical axis. 

We are given the system:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dR}{dt} &= 2R - 0.02RF \\ \frac{dF}{dt} &= 0.01RF - 0.5F \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Configure GDC Graphing Mode
steps.content:
To graph this trajectory, switch your GDC to **Differential Equation** mode. This allows you to input coupled equations where the rate of change depends on both variables.

On most calculators, you will define:
- $x_{1}$ (or $y_{1}$) as $R$
- $x_{2}$ (or $y_{2}$) as $F$

Enter the equations as:
$$\begin{aligned} y'_1 &= 2y_1 - 0.02y_1y_2 \\ y'_2 &= 0.01y_1y_2 - 0.5y_2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Graphing menu, change the graph type to Differential Equation. Enter the system using your calculator's specific syntax (e.g., using x and y or y1 and y2). Set initial conditions to (10, 2).

ti84.instructions: Go to MODE and select DIFF EQUATION. Press Y= and enter Y'1 = 2Y1 - 0.02*Y1*Y2 and Y'2 = 0.01*Y1*Y2 - 0.5*Y2. Set the initial conditions (YI) to Y1=10 and Y2=2.

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a native phase portrait solver for systems, but you can visualize the vector field using a vector field generator or use a specialized ODE solver tool.

calculator.description: Set up the differential equation solver for the system.

steps.title: Set Window and Bounds
steps.content:
Now, we must set the graphing window to match the specific constraints of the question. This includes the time interval and the axis ranges.

- **Time ($t$):** $t_{min} = 0$, $t_{max} = 0.2$
- **Horizontal Axis ($R$):** $5 \le R \le 15$
- **Vertical Axis ($F$):** $1 \le F \le 3$

Also, ensure the initial conditions are set at $t=0$: $R=10$ and $F=2$.

steps.title: Verify Initial Direction
steps.content:
Before looking at the GDC's curve, we can verify the direction of travel at the start point $(10, 2)$. 

For Rabbits:
$$\frac{dR}{dt} = 2(10) - 0.02(10)(2) = 20 - 0.4 = 19.6$$
Since this is positive, $R$ is **increasing** (moving right).

For Foxes:
$$\frac{dF}{dt} = 0.01(10)(2) - 0.5(2) = 0.2 - 1.0 = -0.8$$
Since this is negative, $F$ is **decreasing** (moving down).

steps.title: Sketch the Trajectory
steps.content:
Using the GDC's trace function, we can see the trajectory starts at $(10, 2)$ and moves toward $(14.3, 1.85)$ over the $0.2$-year interval.

When sketching:
1. Mark the start point at $(10, 2)$.
2. Draw a smooth curve that moves to the right (increasing $R$) and slightly downward (decreasing $F$).
3. Stop the curve at approximately $(14.3, 1.85)$.
4. Add an arrow to show the direction of time.

gdc.instructions: View the table of values or use the trace tool to find the coordinate at t = 0.2.

ti84.instructions: Use the TRACE function and type 0.2 for the T value. The screen will display the X (R) and Y (F) coordinates.

desmos.instructions: Not applicable for this specific graphing method.

calculator.description: Verify the endpoint coordinates at t = 0.2.

steps.title: Understand the Goal
steps.content:
We need to sketch the phase portrait trajectory for the rabbit population $R$ and fox population $F$ from $t=0$ to $t=0.2$. 

A phase portrait plots $F$ against $R$ as time $t$ progresses. To do this accurately, we will use **Euler's Method** for coupled systems to approximate the populations at small time steps.

highlights.text: Sketch the phase portrait trajectory for 0 ≤ t ≤ 0.2

steps.title: Set Up Euler's Method
steps.content:
For a coupled system, Euler's method calculates the next values ($R_{n+1}, F_{n+1}$) using the current rates of change and a step size $h$:

$$\begin{aligned} R_{n+1} &= R_n + h \times \left(\frac{dR}{dt}\right)_n \\ F_{n+1} &= F_n + h \times \left(\frac{dF}{dt}\right)_n \end{aligned}$$

We are given the initial conditions at $t=0$:
- $R_0 = 10$
- $F_0 = 2$

We will use a step size of $h = 0.1$ to reach $t=0.2$ in two steps.

steps.title: First Iteration (t = 0.1)
steps.content:
First, calculate the rates of change at $t=0$ where $R=10$ and $F=2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \left(\frac{dR}{dt}\right)_0 &= 2(10) - 0.02(10)(2) = 19.6 \\ \left(\frac{dF}{dt}\right)_0 &= 0.01(10)(2) - 0.5(2) = -0.8 \end{aligned}$$

Now, calculate the populations at $t=0.1$:

$$\begin{aligned} R_1 &= 10 + 0.1(19.6) = 11.96 \\ F_1 &= 2 + 0.1(-0.8) = 1.92 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us our first intermediate point: $(11.96, 1.92)$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expressions directly into the calculator to find R1 and F1.

desmos.instructions: Set R = 10, F = 2 and evaluate R + 0.1(2R - 0.02RF) and F + 0.1(0.01RF - 0.5F).

calculator.description: Calculate the population values at t = 0.1 using the Euler's method iteration.

steps.title: Second Iteration (t = 0.2)
steps.content:
Now, repeat the process using the values from $t=0.1$ ($R=11.96, F=1.92$):

$$\begin{aligned} \left(\frac{dR}{dt}\right)_1 &= 2(11.96) - 0.02(11.96)(1.92) \approx 23.46 \\ \left(\frac{dF}{dt}\right)_1 &= 0.01(11.96)(1.92) - 0.5(1.92) \approx -0.730 \end{aligned}$$

Calculate the populations at $t=0.2$:

$$\begin{aligned} R_2 &= 11.96 + 0.1(23.46) \approx 14.3 \\ F_2 &= 1.92 + 0.1(-0.730) \approx 1.85 \end{aligned}$$

Our final endpoint is approximately $(14.3, 1.85)$.

gdc.instructions: Input the calculation to find the approximate populations at t = 0.2.

ti84.instructions: Use the previous result for R (11.96) and F (1.92) to calculate the next step.

desmos.instructions: Update R and F values and calculate the next coordinates.

calculator.description: Calculate the population values at t = 0.2.

steps.title: Sketch the Trajectory
steps.content:
Now, sketch the trajectory on the axes provided ($5 \le R \le 15$ and $1 \le F \le 3$):

1.  **Start Point:** Plot a solid point at $(10, 2)$.
2.  **End Point:** Plot a point at approximately $(14.3, 1.85)$.
3.  **Curve:** Draw a smooth line connecting these points. Since $R$ is increasing and $F$ is decreasing, the trajectory should curve towards the bottom-right.
4.  **Direction:** Add an arrow to the line to show the movement over time.

parts.content: Find the equilibrium point for this system.
parts.markscheme: - Set derivatives to zero: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} = 0 &\Rightarrow x + 2y - 50 = 0 \\ \dot{y} = 0 &\Rightarrow 2x + y - 40 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Solve for $x$ and $y$: $\Rightarrow x = 10, \, y = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Define the equilibrium conditions
steps.content:
In the context of differential equations, an **equilibrium point** (or steady state) occurs when the variables are no longer changing over time. This means the rates of change, represented by $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, must both be equal to zero.

This concept is a key part of **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.17)** regarding coupled differential equations. To find this point, we start by setting:
$$\dot{x} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{y} = 0$$

steps.title: Set up the linear system
steps.content:
Using the equations provided in the question, we substitute zero for the derivatives to create a system of two linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned} 1) \quad x + 2y - 50 &= 0 \\ 2) \quad 2x + y - 40 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This matches the first step in the markscheme to earn the first method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Solve using substitution
steps.content:
To solve this algebraically, we can isolate $x$ in the first equation and substitute it into the second:

From equation (1):
$$x = 50 - 2y$$

Now, substitute this expression for $x$ into equation (2):
$$\begin{aligned} 2(50 - 2y) + y - 40 &= 0 \\ 100 - 4y + y - 40 &= 0 \\ 60 - 3y &= 0 \\ 3y &= 60 \\ y &= 20 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, find $x$ by plugging $y = 20$ back into our expression for $x$:
$$x = 50 - 2(20) = 10$$

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: In an IB exam, you can use your Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to solve systems of linear equations quickly. This is a great way to double-check your algebraic work.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Equation/Solver menu. Select 'Simultaneous'. Set the number of unknowns to 2. Enter the coefficients: a1=1, b1=2, c1=50 and a2=2, b2=1, c2=40. Press 'Solve'.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS], select 'PlySmlt2', then 'Simult Eqn Solver'. Set equations to 2 and unknowns to 2. Enter the coefficients into the matrix: [[1, 2, 50], [2, 1, 40]]. Press [GRAPH] (SOLVE).

desmos.instructions: Type the two equations into the input bars. The intersection of the two lines is the solution.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations x + 2y = 50 and 2x + y = 40.

steps.title: State the equilibrium point
steps.content:
The equilibrium point for the symbiotic coral populations is:
$$(10, 20)$$

This means that when there are 10 units of population $x$ and 20 units of population $y$, the system is in a state where neither population is growing or shrinking (**A1**).

steps.title: Define the Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
An equilibrium point in a system of differential equations occurs when the rates of change for all variables are zero. This means the population sizes are not changing over time. 

To find this point, we must set the derivatives $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0 \\ \dot{y} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for the Solver
steps.content:
To use the **Simultaneous Equation Solver** on your GDC, the equations need to be in the standard form $ax + by = c$. 

We set our expressions equal to zero and move the constants to the right side:

1.  $x + 2y - 50 = 0 \Rightarrow x + 2y = 50$
2.  $2x + y - 40 = 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y = 40$

This gives us a system of two linear equations with two unknowns ($x$ and $y$). This technique is covered in **Topic 1.8: Use of technology to solve systems of linear equations**.

steps.title: Solve Using GDC
steps.content:
Now, we input the coefficients into the GDC to solve for $x$ and $y$. Our coefficients for the matrix or solver are:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 50 \\ 2 & 1 & 40 \end{pmatrix}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the 'EQUA' (Equation) menu.
2. Select 'Simultaneous' (F1).
3. Select '2' unknowns (F1).
4. Enter the coefficients into the matrix:
   Row 1: [1, 2, 50]
   Row 2: [2, 1, 40]
5. Press 'SOLVE' (F1).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] and select 'PlySmlt2'.
2. Select '2: Simultaneous Eqn Solver'.
3. Set equations to '2' and unknowns to '2'. Press [NEXT] (Graph button).
4. Enter the coefficients: 
   Row 1: 1, 2, 50
   Row 2: 2, 1, 40
5. Press [SOLVE] (Graph button).

desmos.instructions:
In the Matrix Calculator:
1. Create Matrix A: [[1, 2], [2, 1]]
2. Create Matrix B: [[50], [40]]
3. Calculate A⁻¹B to find the values of x and y.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations using the GDC.

steps.title: State the Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
The calculator provides the solution for our system:

$$x = 10, \, y = 20$$

Therefore, the equilibrium point for the symbiotic coral populations is $(10, 20)$. This means that if there are 10 of type $x$ and 20 of type $y$, the populations will remain constant over time.

steps.title: Define Equilibrium
steps.content:
In a system of differential equations, an **equilibrium point** occurs when the variables are no longer changing over time. This means the rates of change, $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, must both be equal to zero.

We start by setting our two equations to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0 \Rightarrow x + 2y - 50 = 0 \\ \dot{y} &= 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y - 40 = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange First Equation
steps.content:
To use the **Graphical Intersection Method**, we need to write our equations in the form $y = f(x)$ so we can enter them into a graphing calculator.

Let's rearrange the first equilibrium equation:

$$\begin{aligned} x + 2y - 50 &= 0 \\ 2y &= 50 - x \\ y &= 25 - 0.5x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange Second Equation
steps.content:
Now, we do the same for the second equilibrium equation to isolate $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + y - 40 &= 0 \\ y &= 40 - 2x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the Intersection
steps.content: Now that we have two functions, $y_1 = 25 - 0.5x$ and $y_2 = 40 - 2x$, we can plot them and find where they cross. This intersection point is the solution that satisfies both equations simultaneously.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = 25 - 0.5x.
3. Enter f2(x) = 40 - 2x.
4. Draw the graph and use the 'Intersection' tool (usually under Analyze Graph or G-Solv).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 25 - 0.5X.
3. Enter Y2 = 40 - 2X.
4. Press [GRAPH]. (Adjust [WINDOW] if needed, e.g., Xmax=50, Ymax=50).
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '5: intersect'.
6. Press [ENTER] for the first curve, [ENTER] for the second curve, and [ENTER] to guess.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 25 - 0.5x into the first expression box.
2. Type y = 40 - 2x into the second expression box.
3. Click on the point where the two lines cross to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the intersection point of the two population lines.

steps.title: State Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
Using the graphing tool, we find that the lines intersect at:

$$x = 10, \, y = 20$$

This means the equilibrium point for the coral populations is $(10, 20)$. This corresponds to the first mark for setting equations to zero and the second mark for the correct values.

This technique relates to **Topic 2.4: Features of a graph**, specifically finding intersection points of different functions.

parts.content: If initially $x = 100$ and $y = 50$, use Euler's method with a time increment of 0.1 to find an approximation for the values of $x$ and $y$ when $t = 1$.
parts.markscheme: - Using Euler's method formulas: $$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \\ t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Calculate values at $t=1$: $x(1) \approx 848, \, y(1) \approx 837$ (3 s.f.) <marks>A1A1</marks>  3 marks total

steps.title: Define Euler's Method Formulas
steps.content:
To approximate the values of $x$ and $y$ in a coupled system of differential equations, we use the iterative Euler's method formulas:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + h \cdot \dot{x}_n \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + h \cdot \dot{y}_n \\ t_{n+1} &= t_n + h \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the given differential equations $\dot{x} = x + 2y - 50$ and $\dot{y} = 2x + y - 40$ with a step size of $h = 0.1$, we get:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify Initial Conditions
steps.content:
We start at $t = 0$ with the following initial populations:

- $x_0 = 100$
- $y_0 = 50$
- $h = 0.1$

Since we want to find the values at $t = 1$, we need to perform 10 iterations (since $10 \times 0.1 = 1$).

steps.title: Calculate First Iteration (t=0.1)
steps.content:
Now we substitute $x_0 = 100$ and $y_0 = 50$ into our formulas to find the values at $t = 0.1$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= 100 + 0.1(100 + 2(50) - 50) \\ &= 100 + 0.1(150) = 115 \\ y_1 &= 50 + 0.1(2(100) + 50 - 40) \\ &= 50 + 0.1(210) = 71 \end{aligned}$$

So at $t = 0.1$, our approximation is $(115, 71)$.

steps.title: Complete Iterations Using Technology
steps.content:
Continuing this manual iterative substitution for 10 steps (until $t = 1.0$) gives the following results:

- $t=0.3: x_3 = 160.69, y_3 = 129.95$
- $t=0.4: x_4 \approx 191.8, y_4 \approx 171.1$
- ...and so on.

After 10 steps, we find:
$$x(1) \approx 847.61...$$
$$y(1) \approx 836.69...$$

gdc.instructions:
Create a recursive formula for two variables. 
Set Initial conditions: u1=100, v1=50.
Set formulas: 
an+1 = an + 0.1*(an + 2*bn - 50)
bn+1 = bn + 0.1*(2*an + bn - 40)
Look for the 11th term (which represents n=10 iterations).

ti84.instructions: Use the 'Sequence' mode in the Y= menu. Set nMin=0, u(n)=u(n-1)+0.1(u(n-1)+2v(n-1)-50), and v(n)=v(n-1)+0.1(2u(n-1)+v(n-1)-40). Set u(nMin)=100 and v(nMin)=50. Go to the table and find n=10.

desmos.instructions: While Desmos isn't ideal for manual recursion like a GDC, you can verify the values using a list or a simple loop calculation.

calculator.description: To avoid manual error, you can use the recursive sequence feature or a spreadsheet-like entry on your GDC.

steps.title: State Final Rounded Answer
steps.content:
Finally, we round our results to 3 significant figures as required by standard IB conventions (unless otherwise specified).

$$x(1) \approx 848$$
$$y(1) \approx 837$$

steps.title: Establish the Euler Formulas
steps.content:
Euler's method for a coupled system uses the gradient (derivative) at the current point to estimate the value at the next point. Given the step size $h = 0.1$, the initial values, and the differential equations, we define the iterative process as follows:

$$\begin{aligned} t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \\ x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \end{aligned}$$

This is consistent with the general Euler formula for coupled systems: 

$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot \dot{x}(x_n, y_n)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot \dot{y}(x_n, y_n)$$

steps.title: Configure GDC Spreadsheet
steps.content:
To avoid manual calculation errors across 10 steps, we use the Spreadsheet application on the GDC. We will set up three columns for $t$, $x$, and $y$. 

1.  In **Row 1**, enter the initial conditions: $t=0, x=100, y=50$.
2.  In **Row 2**, enter the formulas to reference the cells above:
    *   For $t$: `= A1 + 0.1`
    *   For $x$: `= B1 + 0.1 * (B1 + 2 * C1 - 50)`
    *   For $y$: `= C1 + 0.1 * (2 * B1 + C1 - 40)`
3.  **Fill Down** these formulas until you reach $t = 1$. This requires 10 iterations (reaching Row 11).

gdc.instructions:
1. Open Spreadsheet. 
2. Col A: Start at 0, next cell `= a1 + 0.1`. 
3. Col B: Start at 100, next cell `= b1 + 0.1*(b1 + 2*c1 - 50)`. 
4. Col C: Start at 50, next cell `= c1 + 0.1*(2*b1 + c1 - 40)`. 
5. Use 'Fill' command to copy Row 2 down to Row 11.

ti84.instructions:
The TI-84 doesn't have a full spreadsheet, but you can use Lists. 
1. Go to [STAT] -> Edit. 
2. In L1 (for t), enter 0, 0.1, ..., 1. 
3. In L2 (for x) and L3 (for y), you must calculate row-by-row manually or use a small program. 
Iter 1: 100 + 0.1(100+2*50-50) = 115 and 50 + 0.1(2*100+50-40) = 71.
Repeat until t=1.

desmos.instructions:
Use a table. 
1. Add a table with columns t, x, y. 
2. Set initial values in row 1. 
3. Since Desmos doesn't support recursive tables easily, use a script or compute sequentially: x1 = 115, y1 = 71, etc.

steps.title: Identify Values at t = 1
steps.content:
After 10 iterations, we look at the row where $t = 1$ (usually row 11 in the spreadsheet). The calculator provides the following approximate values:

$$x(1) \approx 847.611...$$
$$y(1) \approx 836.690...$$

Rounding these to 3 significant figures as required by IB standards, we get:

$$x(1) \approx 848$$
$$y(1) \approx 837$$

parts.content: Extend this method to conjecture the limit of the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme: - By extending the table, conjecture that: $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{y}{x} = 1$ <marks>M1A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: Show how using the substitution $X = x - 10, \, Y = y - 20$ transforms the system of differential equations into $$\begin{aligned} \dot{X} &= X + 2Y \\ \dot{Y} &= 2X + Y \end{aligned}$$
parts.markscheme: - State derivatives relation: $X = x - 10, \, Y = y - 20 \Rightarrow \dot{X} = \dot{x}, \, \dot{Y} = \dot{y}$ <marks>R1</marks>  - Substitute into original equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{X} &= (X + 10) + 2(Y + 20) - 50 = X + 2Y \\ \dot{Y} &= 2(X + 10) + (Y + 20) - 40 = 2X + Y \end{aligned} $$ <marks>M1A1AG</marks>  3 marks total

parts.content: Solve this system of equations by the eigenvalue method and hence find the general solution for $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}$ of the original system.
parts.markscheme: - Find eigenvalues: $$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 - \lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 1 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow (1 - \lambda)^2 - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = -1 \text{ or } 3 $$ <marks>M1A1A1</marks>  - For $\lambda = -1$: $$ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow q = -p $$ An eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$.  - For $\lambda = 3$: $$ \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow q = p $$ An eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. <marks>M1A1A1</marks>  - General solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1A1</marks>  8 marks total

parts.content: Find the particular solution to the original system, given the initial conditions of part 2.
parts.markscheme: - Substitute $t=0, x=100, y=50$: $$ \begin{aligned} 100 &= A + B + 10 \\ 50 &= -A + B + 20 \end{aligned} \Rightarrow A = 30, \, B = 60 $$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Write particular solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 30 e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + 60 e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: Hence find the exact values of $x$ and $y$ when $t = 1$, giving the answers to 4 significant figures.
parts.markscheme: - Calculate values: $x(1) = 1226, \, y(1) = 1214$ (4 s.f.) <marks>A1A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the general solution
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the investigation (using the eigenvector method), we have derived the general solution for the population of the two coral species. These analytical expressions for $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ are:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= 60e^{3t} + 30e^{-t} + 10 \\ y(t) &= 60e^{3t} - 30e^{-t} + 20 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute the time value
steps.content:
We need to find the populations at $t = 1$. Substitute $t = 1$ into our general equations for $x$ and $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &= 60e^{3(1)} + 30e^{-(1)} + 10 \\ y(1) &= 60e^{3(1)} - 30e^{-(1)} + 20 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: exact values of x and y when t = 1

steps.title: Evaluate population values
steps.content:
Using your GDC, evaluate the exact expressions to find the population values. 

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &= 60e^3 + 30e^{-1} + 10 \approx 1226.168599 \\ y(1) &= 60e^3 - 30e^{-1} + 20 \approx 1214.095832 \end{aligned}$$

The populations after one decade are:

steps.title: Identify system and initial values
steps.content:
To find the values of $x$ and $y$ at $t = 1$, we first need to identify the coupled system of differential equations and the initial populations provided in the earlier parts of this investigation.

The system is:
$$\begin{cases} \dot{x} = x + 2y - 50 \\ \dot{y} = 2x + y - 40 \end{cases}$$

From the previous parts of the question, we use the initial conditions at $t=0$:
$$x(0) = 100, \quad y(0) = 50$$

steps.title: Input into GDC Solver
steps.content:
We will use the numerical differential equation solver on your graphing calculator. This tool calculates values step-by-step using methods like Euler's or Runge-Kutta, which is perfect for coupled systems that are difficult to solve by hand.

You need to set the graph mode to **Differential Equations** and enter the equations as functions of $x$, $y$, and $t$. Note that on many calculators, $x$ and $y$ are represented as $y_1$ and $y_2$.

2. Enter $y1' = y1 + 2y2 - 50$ with $y1(0) = 100$.
3. Enter $y2' = 2y1 + y2 - 40$ with $y2(0) = 50$.
4. Use the table feature ([Ctrl][T]) to see the values at $t=1$.

2. Press [Y=].
3. Set $Y_1' = Y_1 + 2Y_2 - 50$ and $Y_2' = 2Y_1 + Y_2 - 40$.
4. Set initial conditions: $Y_{1i} = 100$ and $Y_{2i} = 50$.
5. Press [2nd][GRAPH] (TABLE) or [2nd][TRACE] (CALC) to find the values at $t=1$.

1. Use the system of ODEs syntax: $x'(t) = x + 2y - 50$ and $y'(t) = 2x + y - 40$.
2. Set the initial points $(x_0, y_0) = (100, 50)$.
3. Look at the table of values generated for $t=1$.

steps.title: State final values
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver, we find the following results at $t=1$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &\approx 1226.168... \\ y(1) &\approx 1214.095... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding these to 4 significant figures as requested, we get:

$$x(1) = 1226, \quad y(1) = 1214$$

parts.content: Use part 6 to find limit of the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme: - Identify dominant term: Dominant term is $60 e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$  - State limit: So $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{y}{x} = 1$ <marks>M1A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: With the initial conditions as given in part 2, determine if the populations converge to the equilibrium values.
parts.markscheme: - State conclusion: The populations do not converge (unstable trajectory). <marks>R1</marks>  1 mark total

parts.content: If instead the initial conditions were given as $x = 20$ and $y = 10$, find the particular solution for $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}$ of the original system, in this case.
parts.markscheme: - Solve for constants: $$ \begin{aligned} 20 &= A + B + 10 \\ 10 &= -A + B + 20 \end{aligned} \Rightarrow A = 10, \, B = 0 $$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Write solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 10 e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: With the initial conditions as given in part 10, determine if the populations converge to the equilibrium values.
parts.markscheme: - Reason about convergence: As $e^{-t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, the populations converge. <marks>R1A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the solution form
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the investigation, the general solution for the populations $x$ and $y$ is expressed as a combination of a constant equilibrium value and exponential terms. The exponents are determined by the eigenvalues of the system, which are $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = 3$.

Given the specific initial conditions in part 10, the solution only contains the decaying exponential term associated with the negative eigenvalue. The general form for the population at time $t$ is:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= 10 + A e^{-t} \\ y(t) &= 20 + B e^{-t} \end{aligned}$$

where 10 and 20 are the equilibrium populations.

steps.title: Evaluate the long-term limit
steps.content:
To determine if the populations converge, we evaluate the limit as time $t$ approaches infinity ($t \to \infty$). 

We know that for any negative exponent, the term approaches zero as the variable grows:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t} = 0$$

As $t$ becomes very large, the parts of the population that vary over time ($A e^{-t}$ and $B e^{-t}$) disappear.

gdc.instructions: Graph the function f(x) = e^(-x). Use the trace tool to observe that as x increases, f(x) approaches the x-axis (y = 0).

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = e^(-X). Press [GRAPH]. Use [TRACE] and scroll to the right to see the Y-value decrease toward zero.

desmos.instructions: Type y = e^(-x) into the expression bar. Observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0 as x increases.

calculator.description: You can verify the behavior of the exponential decay function on your calculator to see it approaching zero.

steps.title: Conclude on convergence
steps.content:
Because the time-dependent terms vanish, the populations will eventually reach a steady state. Specifically, the populations converge to the equilibrium values:

$$\begin{aligned} x &\to 10 \\ y &\to 20 \end{aligned}$$

Since the populations approach fixed constant values, we conclude that **the populations do converge** to the equilibrium values.

steps.title: Identify eigenvalues and equilibrium
steps.content:
To determine the long-term behavior of the coral populations, we first look at the eigenvalues of the system. From the previous parts, we found the eigenvalues to be:

$$\lambda_1 = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad \lambda_2 = -1$$

and the equilibrium point to be $(10, 20)$. In a coupled system, the solution is a combination of exponential terms related to these eigenvalues:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x(t) - 10 \\ y(t) - 20 \end{pmatrix} = c_1 e^{3t} \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$$

where $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$ are the eigenvectors. This system is technically unstable because $\lambda_1 = 3 > 0$, which would normally cause the population to grow away from equilibrium.

steps.title: Analyze initial conditions
steps.content:
The question specifies we use the initial conditions from the previous part. In those conditions, the state of the system lies exactly on the line of the eigenvector associated with the negative eigenvalue ($\lambda_2 = -1$). 

Because the initial state is on this stable eigenvector, the coefficient for the unstable growth term is zero ($c_1 = 0$). This means the general solution simplifies to:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x(t) - 10 \\ y(t) - 20 \end{pmatrix} = c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$$

steps.title: Evaluate the limit
steps.content:
Now we examine what happens as time $t$ increases. For any value of $c_2$, we look at the limit of the exponential term:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t} = 0$$

As $t \to \infty$, the term $c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$ approaches the zero vector. This means the displacement from the equilibrium point disappears over time.

gdc.instructions: Graph y = e^(-x) and use the trace function to see the value as x becomes large.

ti84.instructions: Go to Y= and enter Y1 = e^(-X). Press TABLE (2nd + GRAPH) to see values as X increases.

desmos.instructions: Plot y = e^(-x) and observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0.

calculator.description: Observe how e^(-t) behaves as t increases.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since the only remaining part of the solution decays to zero, the populations $x$ and $y$ will move closer and closer to their equilibrium values of $10$ and $20$.

Therefore, the populations **converge** to the equilibrium values.

parts.content: Calculate $T(2)$ using Euler's method with a step size $h=1$.
parts.markscheme:
- State Euler's method formula:
  $$T_{n+1}=T_n+h \cdot f\left(t_n, T_n\right)$$

- Calculate $f(t_0, T_0)$ with $t_0=0, T_0=20$:
  $$f(0, 20)=0.5(80-20)=30$$

- Apply Euler's method to find $T_1$:
  $$T_1=20+1(30)=50$$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Calculate $f(t_1, T_1)$ with $t_1=1, T_1=50$:
  $$f(1, 50)=0.5(80-50)=15$$

- Apply Euler's method to find $T_2$:
  $$T_2=50+1(15)=65$$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- State final answer:
  $T(2) \approx 65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

parts.content: Solve the differential equation analytically to find the exact solution for $T(t)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Separate variables:
  $$\int \frac{1}{80-T} d T=\int 0.5 d t$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Integrate both sides:
  $$-\ln |80-T|=0.5 t+c_1$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solve for $T$ (letting $A = \pm e^{-c_1}$):
  $$80-T=A e^{-0.5 t}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Apply initial condition $T(0)=20$ to find $A$:
  $$20=80-A \Rightarrow A=60$$

- State the final solution:
  $$T(t)=80-60 e^{-0.5 t}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Determine the percentage error in your approximation from Part 1.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $t=2$ into the exact solution:
  $$T(2)=80-60 e^{-1} \approx 57.93^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the percentage error:
  $$\text { Percentage Error }=\left|\frac{57.93-65}{57.93}\right| \times 100 \approx 12.2 \%$$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: State the limiting temperature as $t \rightarrow \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the behavior as $t \rightarrow \infty$:
  As $t \rightarrow \infty, e^{-0.5 t} \rightarrow 0$, so $T(t) \rightarrow 80$

- Conclude the limiting temperature:
  $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Use implicit differentiation to show that $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y e^{x y}-2 x}{2 y - x e^{x y}}$.
parts.markscheme:
Differentiate $x^{2}+y^{2}=e^{x y}$ with respect to $x$:

$2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x y}\left(y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right)$ <marks>M1A1</marks>

$2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=y e^{x y}+x e^{x y} \frac{d y}{d x}$

Rearranging to isolate $\frac{d y}{d x}$:

$2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-x e^{x y} \frac{d y}{d x}=y e^{x y}-2 x$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\frac{d y}{d x}\left(2 y-x e^{x y}\right)=y e^{x y}-2 x$

$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y e^{x y}-2 x}{2 y - x e^{x y}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Award <marks>M1</marks> for correct application of product rule and chain rule on $e^{xy}$.

parts.content: Use Euler's method with a step size of $h=0.1$ to find the population values $x(0.1)$ and $y(0.1)$ given the initial conditions $x(0)=100$ and $y(0)=20$.
parts.markscheme:
- State Euler's method formulas for the system:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot \frac{dx}{dt} \quad \text{and} \quad y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot \frac{dy}{dt}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

**For population $x(0.1)$:**
- Substitute $x=100, y=20$ into $\frac{dx}{dt}$:
$\frac{dx}{dt} = 2(100) - 0.01(100)(20)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate derivative value:
$\frac{dx}{dt} = 200 - 20 = 180$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply Euler's formula:
$x(0.1) = 100 + 0.1(180)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final value:
$x(0.1) = 118$ <marks>A1</marks>

**For population $y(0.1)$:**
- Substitute $x=100, y=20$ into $\frac{dy}{dt}$:
$\frac{dy}{dt} = -20 + 0.02(100)(20)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate derivative value:
$\frac{dy}{dt} = -20 + 40 = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply Euler's formula:
$y(0.1) = 20 + 0.1(20)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final value:
$y(0.1) = 22$ <marks>A1</marks>

10 marks total

parts.content: Describe the long-term behaviour of $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, with brief justification.
parts.markscheme:
- For the given initial conditions, note that both populations initially increase since $x'(0)=180>0$ and $y'(0)=20>0$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify the equilibrium point by solving $\frac{dx}{dt}=0$ and $\frac{dy}{dt}=0$, giving $(x^*,y^*)=(50,200)$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that when prey are abundant, predators increase; as predator numbers rise, the prey growth term $-0.01xy$ increases in magnitude, so prey eventually stops increasing and begins to decrease. <marks>R1</marks>
- Explain that when prey become scarce, the predator growth term $0.02xy$ reduces and the $-y$ term dominates, so predators subsequently decrease (with a lag relative to prey). <marks>R1</marks>
- State that the Lotka–Volterra model predicts bounded, repeating cycles (closed orbits) around the equilibrium rather than convergence to it. <marks>A1</marks>
- Conclude that the populations oscillate over time with neither population tending to $0$ or to infinity for typical positive initial conditions in this model. <marks>R1</marks>

6 marks total

parts.content: Suggest how changing one or more parameter values in the model might alter the dynamics of the system.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the general equilibrium for $x'=ax-bxy$, $y'=-cy+dxy$:
$(x^*,y^*)=\left(\frac{c}{d},\frac{a}{b}\right)$. Here $(x^*,y^*)=(50,200)$. <marks>A1</marks>
- If the predation coefficient $b$ (currently $0.01$) increases, then $y^*=\frac{a}{b}$ decreases while $x^*=\frac{c}{d}$ is unchanged. <marks>A1</marks>
- If the predator death rate coefficient $c$ (currently $1$) increases, then $x^*=\frac{c}{d}$ increases, meaning a higher prey equilibrium level is required to sustain predators; predators also decline faster when prey are scarce. <marks>A1</marks>
- If the prey growth rate $a$ (currently $2$) increases, then $y^*=\frac{a}{b}$ increases; for small oscillations near equilibrium the linearized angular frequency is $\omega=\sqrt{ac}$, so increasing $a$ increases the frequency (decreases the period) near the equilibrium. <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total
